item.title: About
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
Welcome to Early Reflections, a space where I’ll be exploring issues in Buddhist philosophy. My own background is in Western philosophy of language, philosophy of mind and epistemology, with a special interest in the revival of Kant and Hegel within analytic philosophy. One of the central aims of this project is to read these traditions against each other.

I first came into contact with Buddhist philosophy when a thread of argument relating to illusionism and the hard problem of consciousness led me to Jay Garfield’s work on Nāgārjuna. From there I became interested in Madhyamaka, Yogācāra, the epistemology of Dharmakīrti, the various disputes between and syntheses of these schools, Abhidharma, and ultimately the whole philosophical milieu that Buddhism emerged from and exists in. It remains fascinating to me how so many of the questions that recur in Buddhist philosophy—especially in the Indian Mahayana—foreshadow the main themes of early modern European philosophy.
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to Early Reflections, a space where I’ll be exploring issues in Buddhist philosophy. My own background is in Western philosophy of language, philosophy of mind and epistemology, with a special interest in the revival of Kant and Hegel within analytic philosophy. One of the central aims of this project is to read these traditions against each other.

I first came into contact with Buddhist philosophy when a thread of argument relating to illusionism and the hard problem of consciousness led me to Jay Garfield’s work on Nāgārjuna. From there I became interested in Madhyamaka, Yogācāra, the epistemology of Dharmakīrti, the various disputes between and syntheses of these schools, Abhidharma, and ultimately the whole philosophical milieu that Buddhism emerged from and exists in. It remains fascinating to me how so many of the questions that recur in Buddhist philosophy—especially in the Indian Mahayana—foreshadow the main themes of early modern European philosophy.

I am also a meditator. When I first started meditating it was not particularly connected with my interest in philosophy, Buddhist or otherwise. Over time, however, these two streams have converged as I have developed more familiarity with the Dharma and become increasingly persuaded that it offers a comprehensive and rational framework for living. The fragments posted here accordingly constitute one limb of a broader practice. They represent my own efforts to find my way about in the Dharma.

That said my aim here is to consider various facets of the Dharma through the unashamedly heterodox lens of Western analytic rationalism, not to give a faithful exposition of the Buddhist worldview. If it seems to me that Kant and the Buddha have a disagreement, I come with no prior commitments about who must win the argument. Then again I don’t believe either would want me to, and I find no inherent contradiction between the soteriological ambitions underlying the Buddhist cultivation of Right View and the more Promethean impulses that are part and parcel of the philosophical legacy we inherit from Greece. My only promise is that I shall try to be brief and not waffle on too much. Less brief than Nāgārjuna, perhaps, but certainly more so than Hegel.

Sam

item.title: Bibliography
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
Bibliography

  • B. Bodhi, In the buddha’s words: An anthology of discourses from the pali canon. Somerville, MA: Wisdom Publications, 2005.
  • R. Brandom, A spirit of trust: A reading of hegel’s phenomenology. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 2019.
  • R. Burbea, Seeing that frees: Meditations on emptiness and dependent arising. United Kingdom: Hermes Amara, 2014.
  • Candrakīrti, Introduction to the middle way: Chandrakirti’s madhyamakavatara with commentary. Boston: Shambhala Publications, 2002.
  • P. Churchland, Reduction, qualia, and the direct introspection of brain states, in Journal of philosophy, , 1985, vol. 82, pp. 8–28. [PDF]
  • B. Connelly, Inside vasubandhu’s yogacara: A practitioner’s guide. Boston: Shambhala Publications, 2016.
  • G. Ferraro, A criticism of m. Siderits and j. L. Garfield’s “semantic interpretation” of nāgārjuna’s theory of two truths, Asian Philosophy, vol. 23, no. 3, pp. 251–269, 2013.
  • P. Feyerabend, Against method. London: Verso, 1975.
  • S. Kripke, Wittgenstein on rules and private language: An elementary exposition. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1982.
  • Nāgārjuna, The fundamental wisdom of the middle way: Nāgārjuna’s mūlamadhyamakakārikā. New York: Oxford University Press, 1995.
  • F. Nietzsche, The antichrist. London: Penguin Books, 1895.
  • M. Siderits, Buddhism as philosophy: An introduction. Aldershot, UK: Ashgate, 2007.
  • W. Waldron, Making sense of mind-only: Why yogācāra buddhism matters. Honolulu: University of Hawai’i Press, 2003.
  • L. Wittgenstein, Philosophical investigations. Oxford: Blackwell, 1953.

item.title: Note on the Tension Theory of Craving
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
What I’m calling a ‘tension theory of craving’ here is something I’d imagine is familiar to anyone who’s spent any time in Buddhist meditation circles. It is the idea that craving and aversion are manifestations of sanskaras understood as pockets of trapped tension.

The attraction of this idea is clear, and it certainly chimes with the basic meditative experience. Cravings arise as contractions of mind and body; by noticing them, disidentifying with them, and relaxing around them the meditator encourages them to dissipate their energy. But the tension theory goes a step beyond this to claim that this relaxing around tension is itself the releasing of the sanskara. This is a claim I’ve heard most often in vipassana and other neo-Theravada contexts, sometimes leading people to see a close affinity between Buddhist meditation and Western practices such as somatic experiencing and TRE, which aim to induce various kinds of somatic release in the treatment of trauma.
item.content:encoded:
What I’m calling a ‘tension theory of craving’ here is something I’d imagine is familiar to anyone who’s spent any time in Buddhist meditation circles. It is the idea that craving and aversion are manifestations of sanskaras understood as pockets of trapped tension.

The attraction of this idea is clear, and it certainly chimes with the basic meditative experience. Cravings arise as contractions of mind and body; by noticing them, disidentifying with them, and relaxing around them the meditator encourages them to dissipate their energy. But the tension theory goes a step beyond this to claim that this relaxing around tension is itself the releasing of the sanskara. This is a claim I’ve heard most often in vipassana and other neo-Theravada contexts, sometimes leading people to see a close affinity between Buddhist meditation and Western practices such as somatic experiencing and TRE, which aim to induce various kinds of somatic release in the treatment of trauma.

What I’d like to point out is that we don’t need to see sanskaras this way at all. In the first instance a sanskara is a habit—a disposition to act on craving conditioned by past behaviour, a disposition which only becomes active when it comes into contact with particular triggers. It is certainly true that tension will accompany the triggering of a sanskara and the consequent arising of craving, and moreover that relaxing around this tension can be of great use in arresting the reflexive identification with craving that empowers it as action.

But this does not mean the tension was somehow there all along, and nor does it mean that relaxing around the craving will make it go away. Sometimes it does, of course—this is often the case with small hinderances that arise in meditation. Less so for the bigger cravings and aversions, when much stronger psychological forces are in play. Often the reality is that no matter how much we relax around contraction, sitting with craving can be extremely uncomfortable. This is OK, because ultimately it is not something waiting to be released, but a conditional response that is being reconditioned. Discomfort should be expected, perhaps lots of it. But if you have a tension theory in the back of your mind, the persistance of discomfort even in the relaxation of all apparent tension could easily become confusing or disheartening.

Ultimately the tension theory seems to reify sanskaras. But sanskaras are not concrete things, not pockets of stuff trapped somewhere in our psyches. They are latent in our minds only in the sense that a disposition to melt is latent in a block of iron—the disposition becomes active (iron melts, craving arises) only in response to contact with a triggering stimulus. This is just a brief note, but something I wanted to jot down as it highlights both the subtle way in which reifying tendencies can infiltrate the understanding of meditative experience, and some of the practical consequences of this.

item.title: Insight as Skill
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: One of the elements I find most attractive in Rob Burbea’s approach to dharma practice is the way he defines insight in terms of effect rather than content. An insight, he says, is any way of looking at experience that releases clinging in the moment1. This carefully sidesteps a common but unhelpful tendency to view the meditative journey as a progression through a series of waystations defined as distinct experiential states, some of which promise to leave our perception of self and world altered forever. Instead the path is to be seen as something more like the acquisition of a bag of tricks, a gradual accumulation of heterogenous bits of know-how together yielding an ever greater capacity for skillful responsiveness.
item.content:encoded:
One of the elements I find most attractive in Rob Burbea’s approach to dharma practice is the way he defines insight in terms of effect rather than content. An insight, he says, is any way of looking at experience that releases clinging in the moment1. This carefully sidesteps a common but unhelpful tendency to view the meditative journey as a progression through a series of waystations defined as distinct experiential states, some of which promise to leave our perception of self and world altered forever. Instead the path is to be seen as something more like the acquisition of a bag of tricks, a gradual accumulation of heterogenous bits of know-how together yielding an ever greater capacity for skillful responsiveness.

All for the better insight, I’d say. Because one thing that is clear is that experiences are cheap these days, even (especially?) peak experiences. You can just fly to Peru and buy one. Or better, find some back-alley neurobiologist prepared to apply a carefully targeted electrical stimulus to your brain—contrary to expectation, ecstatic or mystical experiences have turned out to be some of the easiest to induce through direct electrical stimulation. And it is well-known that episodes of mania or psychosis are often accompanied by extremely strong experiential states of subjective revelation, even where the sense of revelation attaches to delusional beliefs. An even more dramatic example is found in Cotard’s delusion, a rare psychiatric condition in which the sufferer insists with absolute conviction that they are dead or do not exist (apparently with the same degree of self-certainty that led Descartes to insist that he did).

These examples are not intended to call into question the value of peak experiences, but they do cast doubt on their epistemic status. What they show is that there is something like a phenomenology of knowingness, a felt sense of knowing detachable from any particular known content, and which has no trouble at all attaching to false or even contradictory content. The implication of this detachability is that the mere fact of being in certain experiential state cannot in itself furnish the meditator with justification for any claims the experience might incline them to make about reality, the nature of consciousness, or anything else.

This is not to say that insight has no experiential component—certainly it does. But insight cannot simply be something one has. It must also be something one reflects upon, articulates, discusses with others, cross-references, practices, and ultimately consolidates as a kind of skill. What is required is something like a process of mutual calibration between first-person experience and third-person reflection, which could perhaps be likened to the training of a wine taster. No-one would claim it’s possible to become a proficient wine taster by merely learning about the chemistry of wine without ever actually tasting it. Yet the chemical knowledge does play a crucial role in the process, and cannot be dispensed with. By repeatedly and systematically matching chemical elements in the known composition of particular wines to particular flavours and aromas known experientially through tasting, the aspiring wine taster gradually learns to discern the presence of particular compounds and contrasts—they learn, for instance, to (quite literally) taste the phenolic content.

There is a sense in which the casual wine drinker tastes the phenolic content too, but only in the dim sense the phenolic content contributes to the overall flavour. They don’t taste the phenolic content as phenolic content; they are unable to pick it out as a distinct element in the mix. A point Paul Churchland once made about this is that it is not only that the experienced wine taster possesses a skill which most of us do not [1]. It seems also that through their acquisition of this skill the wine taster has in fact transformed the content of their experience. The experience of drinking wine is not to them what it is to the casual drinker, just as the astronomer’s experience of the night sky is different to the non-astronomer’s and the professional conductor hears something in the symphony most of us cannot. The conceptual discernment that comes through training makes possible a depth and richness of experience inaccessible without it.

Churchland then asks us to imagine a hypothetical future in which neurobiology has entered its ‘utopian’ era: our knowledge of the brain is complete and precise and technologies allowing us to look inside it to arbitrary levels of detail are cheap. He points out that in this situation we would in principle have the tools to each become the master tasters of our own inner lives, using those utopian brain sciences and technologies to train ourselves to reliably identify even the most subtle shadings of emotion in reference to the specific brain events they correlate with. Far from the devaluation of inner life many anticipate in the steady encroachment of neurobiology into the domain of first-person experience, according to Churchland the effect of this would be to open up vast new vistas of inner experience. Ultimately it would allow us to dispense with clumsy ‘folk psychological’ notions like boredom or grief, to be replaced by a neurobiological vocabulary enabling much finer discernments of conscious experience, and so, as with the astronomer looking up at the night sky, making possible new depths of appreciation, new modes of response.

Whatever your instincts may be with respect to this scenario, it makes vivid a certain interplay between theory and experience in the acquisition of a particular kind of skill, which necessarily brings with it a transformation of experience. This makes it helpful for thinking about the kind of transformation that insight is supposed to induce and how it might work. Insight practices are certainly not about mapping experiential qualities onto neurological correlates. They are rather about discerning the workings of a distinctive process, one which is always active in consciousness and in the construction of perceptual contents, yet which is at the same time hidden from consciousness on account of its reflexive identification with those very contents. And it is for this reason that insight is usually understood as beginning with the cultivation of skillful disidentification, in one form or another.

What the present line of thought helps us to see is that insight, which in the Burbea framing has a kind of counterfactual structure—if this way of looking is applied, it brings about that loosening—can be regarded as a skillset developed in the course meditative experience rather than as an experiential state in itself, and moreover how this illuminates rather than obscures the potential of insight to radically transform our experience of self and world.

  1. See e.g. Chapter 4 of Seeing that Frees. [2] ↩︎

item.title: Note on Immanence (or, Conventional Truth Without Ultimate Reality)
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
In this post I’d like to think a bit about the distinction between ultimate and conventional truth, and to consider to what extent these different kinds of truth should be taken as corresponding to ultimate and conventional levels of reality. This is a doctrinal issue which receives different treatments in different places, and reflecting on it can help clarify some of the more paradoxical teachings in the later tradition.

In the Abhidharma, ultimate and conventional levels of truth map onto ultimate and conventional levels of reality in a straightforward way. According to this framework the only ultimately real entities are dharmas, and ultimate truth is just the accurate description of these dharmas. The objects and phenomena picked out in conventional designations (tables, trees, emotions, selves, thoughts) lack ultimate reality because they are mere aggregates, perceptual and conceptual fictions overlaying the ever-flowing stream of dharmas. Nonetheless we may still speak of a difference between truth and falsehood at the conventional level, because some of these fictions are useful and others are not. The utility of conventional designations is unpacked in terms of whether or not they line up with the clusters of dharmas on which they are imposed. Ultimate truths explain what counts as conventional truth, and facts at the ultimate level underwrite the possibility of error at the conventional level.
item.content:encoded:
In this post I’d like to think a bit about the distinction between ultimate and conventional truth, and to consider to what extent these different kinds of truth should be taken as corresponding to ultimate and conventional levels of reality. This is a doctrinal issue which receives different treatments in different places, and reflecting on it can help clarify some of the more paradoxical teachings in the later tradition.

In the Abhidharma, ultimate and conventional levels of truth map onto ultimate and conventional levels of reality in a straightforward way. According to this framework the only ultimately real entities are dharmas, and ultimate truth is just the accurate description of these dharmas. The objects and phenomena picked out in conventional designations (tables, trees, emotions, selves, thoughts) lack ultimate reality because they are mere aggregates, perceptual and conceptual fictions overlaying the ever-flowing stream of dharmas. Nonetheless we may still speak of a difference between truth and falsehood at the conventional level, because some of these fictions are useful and others are not. The utility of conventional designations is unpacked in terms of whether or not they line up with the clusters of dharmas on which they are imposed. Ultimate truths explain what counts as conventional truth, and facts at the ultimate level underwrite the possibility of error at the conventional level.

This account has two components. The first is an ontology: dharmas are real, everything else is mental projection which may or may not line up with what’s going on for real. The second is the idea that truth is accurate description, in particular that ultimate truth is accurate description of ultimate reality. These two components add up to a fairly standard kind of reductionism, in which the ultimate reality of the dharmas serves as a non-conventional (or transcendent) ground securing the objectivity of the conventional world of appearances.

There are many ways of approaching the break with the Abhidharma represented by Nagarjuna, but one fruitful way in is to view it as a radical rethinking of this relationship between truth and reality. Of course Nagarjuna, following the Perfection of Wisdom literature, rejected the ultimate reality of dharmas, instead establishing universal emptiness as the ultimate truth. But it is essential to this account that emptiness not be taken as a competing description of ultimate reality. The claim of universal emptiness should be seen rather as a rejection of any attempt to give a positive account of an ultimate reality which underwrites the world of conventional appearance. The ‘emptiness of emptiness’—the climax of Nagarjuna’s analysis1—makes clear that this is not intended as a new account of ultimate reality, but a rejection of the assumption that conventional truth must be underwritten by an account of the way reality is in itself.

This is sometimes called the ‘semantic interpretation’ of Nagarjuna’s theory of the two truths, which Mark Siderits has glossed as the idea that ‘the ultimate truth is that there is no ultimate truth’2. This rather glib formulation can be put less paradoxically by saying that the ultimate truth is that there is no ultimate reality. The natural question arising here is, what then happens to conventional truth? The Abdhidharma finds a transcendent ground of conventional truth in the ultimate reality of dharmas. Nagarjuna appears to reject the very idea of a transcendent ground, so what, we might ask, could possibly make sense of the idea that some judgements couched in conventional terms are true while others are not? What makes a useful fiction useful, if not that it tracks some feature of things as they really are? What accounts for the objective content of conventional appearances? If no good answers to these questions can be found, then the charge that Nagarjuna’s position entails a form of nihilism would seem to be vindicated.

One answer, which seems to be standard in the tradition, is that this notion of a ‘useful fiction’ is just where the chain of explanation bottoms out. If the Abhidharma wants to explain the utility of conventional belief in terms of ultimate truths about dharmas, then the proposed alternative is that we just explain conventional truth in terms of conventional utility. It is common to see it written, for example, that conventional entities are like illusions. The only difference between ‘real’ water and a mirage in the desert is that the former quenches thirst while the latter does not. Some fictions are useful, others are not—there simply is nothing else to say about their truth, since utility is all that truth amounts to.3

The problem with this is that the concept of utility doesn’t seem to contain the resources needed to pin down what we mean by truth. No matter how useful a belief is, I can always imagine a situation in which it is false despite this utility. Indeed, I can even entertain the possibility that at least some of my own beliefs are both useful and false. And if this is so then when I take it that some judgement is true, I cannot simply mean by this that it would be useful to believe. A proponent of the view might object that it is not intended to provide an analysis of the meaning of truth, but just that considerations of utility—including, perhaps, indirect considerations such as the development of our cognitive faculties as evolutionary adaptations—are what ultimately explain what it is we are doing when we call a belief true. But even so, on such an account it will always be possible to construct scenarios in which utility and truth come apart. Ultimately this route seems unable to provide a non-relativistic account of conventional truth, in which case it cannot claim to have secured the objectivity of the conventional and countered the charge of nihilism.

This brief sketch of what goes wrong with a utility theory gestures to a more general concern. If we reject the idea that the objective content of conventional appearance is determined by a transcendent ground, yet want to hold on to the idea that there is such a content, then we are left searching for something like an ‘immanent ground’. In practice this means that rather than explaining truth in terms of some correspondence between our conventional beliefs and the way things really (i.e. ultimately) are, we will have to explain it in terms which remain within the bounds of the conventional. But this is always going to create problems. Given any conventional belief and any set of conventional considerations which determine it as true, it will always be possible to produce further conventional considerations which undermine that determination. In essence, this is a version of what in philosophy of science is known as the Duhem-Quine thesis: given any set of observations it is always possible to construct multiple conflicting theories which adequately explain them. The whole reason to posit an ultimate reality—a way things are in themselves, independently of our conventional designations—is that it serves as a final tribunal before which disputes between competing conventional accounts can be settled. Without such a tribunal it seems the proliferation of conflicting conventional ‘truths’ can always proceed unchecked.

If there can be neither a transcendent nor an immanent ground of conventional truth, does that not mean that the tradition downstream from Nagarjuna cannot make good on its claim that conventional truth survives the analysis of universal emptiness? I think there’s a move that can be made here. It is not one I’ve ever encountered in a Buddhist context, but it is a key idea in German Idealism, and in Hegelian dialectics in particular. The trick is to exploit a certain asymmetry in the notions of truth and error. When interpreting an ancient text, for example, we may concede that it is impossible to ever reach its ‘true’ meaning. In this sense it is essentially open to reinterpretation. But this does not render it meaningless, and what seems to underlie this intuition is the fact that while we may never be able to settle the question of its unique true meaning, it is perfectly possible to identify wrong or implausible readings. In this respect, while interpretations can always proliferate this cannot be described as an unchecked proliferation—it is governed by an objective logic according to which erroneous readings are uncovered and discarded. Without ever making reference to a true meaning that transcends interpretation, the structure of a space of possible meanings is progressively articulated in the ongoing dialogue between conflicting interpretations.

It may not be possible to locate an immanent ground of truth, but perhaps all that is needed to avert nihilism is something more like an immanent condition of error. In Hegel’s system this immanent condition is given by the notion of internal contradiction. We do not determine what is true in our conventional experience by comparing it with an ultimate reality—this latter is a fiction (or in more modern terms: the evidence is always contaminated by theory). Instead what happens is that the experience of error manifests through internal contradictions in our total conventional theory of reality. Contradictions are then resolved through a process of reinterpretation in which the error is integrated (typically by uncovering and discarding the implicit assumptions that produced the contradiction, and/or by producing new interpretive hypotheses). There is no ultimate reality—a transcendent ground, a language in which the world speaks. Reality has a radically open texture. Yet this need not lead to the nihilistic conclusion that all conventional theories are arbitrary. Reality is always open to reinterpretation, but the integration of error provides this process with an objective constraint in the generation and resolution of internal contradiction.

Clearly this departs from accounts of emptiness offered by the tradition. Yet it sometimes seems to me that these accounts can vacillate between a ‘thin’ conception of emptiness which makes no difference at all to our conventional experience, undermining the claim that universal emptiness is a radical proposition with liberating potential, and a ‘thicker’ conception which, while certainly radical, never adequately responds to the charge of nihilism. While the quick sketch above leaves many questions unanswered, it seems to me to at least gesture in the direction of a resolution to this problem.

  1. See chapter 24 of Nagarjuna’s Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, “Analysis of the Four Noble Truths.” [1] ↩︎

  2. See Siderits’ 2007 book Buddhism as Philosophy, especially the chapter on the two truths [2]. The semantic interpretation is not uncontroversial—see, e.g., Giuseppe Ferraro’s criticism [3]—but I won’t consider that dispute here. The rest of this post will not mean much to anyone who subscribes to a realist interpretation of Nagarjuna’s theory of the two truths. ↩︎

  3. Something like this seems to be expressed by Chandrakirti when he says that “Everything the six unimpaired senses grasp and perceive within everyday experience of the world is true from the perspective of the world; the rest the world itself defines as false.” Madhyamakāvatāra 6.25 [4] ↩︎

item.title: Akrasia and the Bodhisattva Ideal
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
A familiar stereotype underlying the pejorative ‘Hinayana’ framing of the non-Mahayana schools is the monk who withdraws from the world to selfishly pursue enlightenment for themselves. According to this framing the Mahayana distinguishes itself from the Hinayana through its emphasis on compassion as the primary goal of the path.

This stereotype is undermined somewhat by the fact that even according to the most world-transcending early portrayals of nirvana, the cultivation of moral perfection, which certainly includes compassion, is regarded as necessary for enlightenment. Any monk who was genuinely withdrawn from the suffering of others would thereby be incapable of achieving enlightenment, by Hinayana lights too. In terms of outcomes, then, there appears to be little difference between the two vehicles.
item.content:encoded:
A familiar stereotype underlying the pejorative ‘Hinayana’ framing of the non-Mahayana schools is the monk who withdraws from the world to selfishly pursue enlightenment for themselves. According to this framing the Mahayana distinguishes itself from the Hinayana through its emphasis on compassion as the primary goal of the path.

This stereotype is undermined somewhat by the fact that even according to the most world-transcending early portrayals of nirvana, the cultivation of moral perfection, which certainly includes compassion, is regarded as necessary for enlightenment. Any monk who was genuinely withdrawn from the suffering of others would thereby be incapable of achieving enlightenment, by Hinayana lights too. In terms of outcomes, then, there appears to be little difference between the two vehicles.

Nevertheless there is clearly something at stake in the Boddhisattva ideal. My view is that the dispute really concerns a more foundational metaethical question, namely: why should we be moral at all? If the Hinayana answer is that you should cultivate moral concern for others because it will help you achieve enlightenment, then the Mahayana could be interpreted as rejecting this answer, instead urging that moral concern must be cultivated as an end in itself.

Given outcomes are the same, you could be forgiven for wondering why this question matters. But while theoretically the destination may be the same, the answer may have important implications for how moral motivation works in practice. Indeed, the whole reason it might seem like a good idea to find self-interested reasons to cultivate moral virtue is that they provide a practitioner with concrete, personal reasons to pursue a moral project which may otherwise seem abstract. Perhaps, then, we can see the Boddhisattva ideal as arising out of a growing suspicion that this strategy is flawed. More explicitly, the suspicion might be that self-interested motivations for cultivating moral virtue are self-defeating.

Why might we believe this? One immediate reason is that life routinely throws up situations in which self-interest and moral concern are at odds with one another. Even armed with the observation that the cultivation of moral concern makes us happier (a fact now confirmed by empirical psychology), this logic has limits—many situations arise in which morality demands a degree of self-sacrifice impossible to justify in terms of some hypothetical renumeration in happiness or spiritual growth. In fact it is only in these situations we’d typically consider there to be something genuinely moral on the line.

But there’s another, more fundamental reason to reject self-interested justifications for morality. To illustrate, consider a response to the above problem based on a conception of ‘expanded’ self-interest. We might think here of Vedic conceptions of self in which one’s own personal identity is equated (via the intermediary notion of Atman) with the whole of reality or Brahman. On this view moral concern for others is justified on the basis that they already are you, in some suitably foundational and metaphysical sense. From the perspective of the ultimate, their needs and preferences are subsumed into your own sphere of prudential self-interest.

This route may seem promising, as it seems to offer a simple and direct reason to care about the suffering of others. But consider how it might regard conflicts of interest. Since according to this model a conflict of interest between two people is (from the ultimate perspective) a conflict between two parts of the same self, the situation is analogous to a conflict of desire within a single person. Say I decide to quit smoking on Monday, then on Friday find myself reaching for a cigarette. How do I decide which desire to act on? One approach is to suggest there can only be one ‘true’ desire, and resolving the conflict is simply a matter of improving one’s self-knowledge. But this amounts to the claim that the conflict is illusory, a position which if dubious in the case of conflicting desire will only become more so when widened to moral conflict in general.

Another common approach is to view resolution of the conflict as a matter of will-power. But this misunderstands the problem, which is not about the relative strength of the conflicting desires. It is rather a logical problem arising from the fact that there is only one locus of account. By recognising my desire to stop smoking on Monday I effectively place myself under a rule: to refrain from smoking even if the desire arises. The problem is that the same authority with which I bind myself to that rule can then be used to nullify or reinterpret it later. So when the desire to smoke inevitably does arise I have no real reason to discount it in favour of my original desire to quit. Viewed this way, it can be seen that the problem is not so much that my capacity to self-legislate is too weak, but that it is too strong. In practice, if the force of a reason (to act or refrain from acting) derives from immediate self-legislation then it seems we will never have good reason to do anything other than act on whichever desire is immediately present. This is regardless of whether it conflicts with other desires we’ve had at other times, or with higher aspirations we may associate with our sense of ourselves as beings extended in time.

This has sometimes been called the problem of akrasia. This word was first used by philosophers in ancient Greece who were perplexed about how it is possible that a person could ever act against their own better judgement. But what the above line of thought actually points to is the opposite problem, namely how it is possible that anyone ever restrains their immediate impulses in the pursuit of long term goals. The lesson usually taken from this argument—which originated with Wittgenstein and was later developed by Saul Kripke1—is that the normative force of a rule, and hence of reasons in general, always depends on its being articulated within a community of rule followers. If there is only a single rule follower the content of the rule will be inherently unstable. What seems to be required is the recognition of others’ authority to hold you to the commitments you take upon yourself.

This insight into the social nature of rule-following and the normative force of reasons both shows why an Atman-based account of expanded self-interest cannot provide an adequate response to the metaethical question, and points the way to a better answer. The problem is that by assimilating moral concern into the prudential sphere of a single universal self it generates a problem analogous to akrasia, in effect guaranteeing that genuine moral conflicts can never be resolved in practice (parties to a conflict can never provide one another with a reason to compromise). But while it shows that self-interest cannot be used to underpin moral concern, even when universalised, it also hints that the opposite may be possible. If our capacity to take ourselves as autonomous rational subjects always depends on our embedding within a community who mutually reinforce and stabilise the content of the rules we each place on ourselves, then this indicates that something like a moral sense—the mutual recognition of distinct others as ends in themselves—is logically prior to any desires, preferences or goals indexed to individual, temporally extended selves.

Let’s come back to the Bodhisattva ideal. I’ve suggested that this development within the Buddhist tradition might be seen as the rejection of a certain answer to a distinctively metaethical question. But once looked at this way, we can also see that it doesn’t really provide its own answer. If asked why we should be moral, the Bodhisattva ideal just says yes. The line of thought I’ve been exploring here gestures towards a more substantial answer to this question. This answer is broadly Kantian: it says that the authority of morality derives directly from the structure of our own rational agency. More explicitly, it says that insofar as we take ourselves to have any reasons at all—including our own self-interested desires and preferences—we are already implicitly committed to moral reasons to act or refrain from acting, whether or not we acknowledge this commitment explicitly. The further (ultimately Hegelian) insight that practical agency is a social achievement makes explicit how the moral sense—the recognition of other sentient beings as sources of reasons which obligate your action—is logically prior to rational self-interest.

  1. Introduced in the Philosophical Investigations [1], Wittgenstein first presented this as an argument intended to show that a language spoken by just one person is impossible. It was later developed into a general rule-following paradox by Kripke in Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language [2]. ↩︎

item.title: From Impermanence to Emptiness
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: In this post I want to jot down a few thoughts on the relationship between impermanence and emptiness. This is inspired by some comments Rob Burbea makes in Seeing That Frees1, which came as something of a revelation as they articulate clearly something I’ve felt at an intuitive but murky level for some time. These concern the limitations of using impermanence as a primary framework for insight, and how a reorientation around emptiness can help move beyond these limitations. Implicitly, then, this is about issues right at the root of the origins of the Mahayana. But in typical Burbea fashion it is framed in a way that has direct relevance for practice.
item.content:encoded:
In this post I want to jot down a few thoughts on the relationship between impermanence and emptiness. This is inspired by some comments Rob Burbea makes in Seeing That Frees1, which came as something of a revelation as they articulate clearly something I’ve felt at an intuitive but murky level for some time. These concern the limitations of using impermanence as a primary framework for insight, and how a reorientation around emptiness can help move beyond these limitations. Implicitly, then, this is about issues right at the root of the origins of the Mahayana. But in typical Burbea fashion it is framed in a way that has direct relevance for practice.

In some traditional framings of the three characteristics of conditioned existence, impermanence is presented as the master concept that explains the other two. Phenomena are understood to be unsatisfactory because they are impermanent. Anattā is understood as the recognition that there is no fixed or permanent self. But on closer inspection these understandings may appear a little flimsy. Does craving really depend on a perception of permanence? If I feel the need to reach for a beer after work, is the force of this compulsion really predicated on a sense—conscious or unconscious—that any pleasure or relief it might provide will persist beyond the immediate future? It is true that observing a desire as impermanent and subject to change can loosen its grip, but this observation can cut both ways. I have certainly had experiences where bringing the standpoint of impermanence to craving has actually seemed to increase its solidity and momentum—after all, if the craving is impermanent then so are the consequences of acting on it.

Another example can help pin down what’s at stake in this. Say you have a romantic infatuation that drags on and on. For various reasons—perhaps it is not reciprocated, or is reciprocated inconsistently—it becomes a whole cursed tangle of a thing, involving much dissatisfaction and pain. But one day it just kind of lifts, like the curtains at the end of a long and tragic play, and you are left looking at this person thinking ugh, this is just a normal plain old boring person with normal problems and normal warts. What was I thinking?

You could look at this experience through the lens of impermanence. The story here might be something like: there was an object of desire and then separate to that there was a desire, and while the desire may have seemed very solid and important while it was present in the end it was just a passing cloud. But while this wouldn’t be wrong, it does seem to miss something crucial in this experience. Because it is not just that the desire passes while the object of desire remains the same—in this moment when the curtain lifts, the object of desire is itself revealed to be something other than it had been taken to be. The real revelation here is that the object of desire was always a construct of sorts, never just this particular person but also your own mental imputation externalised in them. If the deep structure of clinging involves this kind of fabrication of the very objects which are clung to, then an impermanence way of looking that doesn’t explicitly target this fabrication will have limited application in uncovering and unbinding clinging in its gross and subtle forms.

And it is this point which moves us into the terrain of emptiness, because insight into emptiness consists precisely in this recognition of the fabricated nature of perceptual objects. To say that the self as such is a fabrication devoid of substantial basis is to say something much stronger than that it is impermanent and subject to change. This is not just philosophical nitpicking, but potentially has deep implications for how the path itself is interpreted. There is, for instance, a not uncommon idea of spiritual development which is explicitly predicated on the idea that the self is not fixed or permanent—it is, therefore, something that can change and grow. Yet this idea can nevertheless operate under a more subtle kind of reification of self, in which the various dimensions of skill, experience, and integration involved in practice are implicitly collapsed down into a single developmental scale. Such an idea can produce a sense that, say, proficiency in meditation and insight should also be markers for other forms of psychological integration. This idea may not be endorsed explicitly but may rather operate in the background, as when people find it hard to understand how a dharma teacher who is undoubtedly very wise and experienced could also be implicated in sexual misconduct.

It might be argued that the impermanence framing already incorporates the notion that phenomena lack substantial basis, because to posit a substantial basis is to posit an unchanging medium in which the various changing properties of an object are unified. But to make this argument is to interpret impermanence through the lens of emptiness. This is certainly not a bad thing, and indeed it helps us to see how an impermanence way of looking might fit within a broader emptiness practice.

But when impermanence becomes the core focus of practice things can take quite a different direction. It is well documented that attending to the aspect of change in experience can produce a refinement of attention into an ever more fine-grained, microscopic beam which hones in on the most minute detail of experience. The standpoint of impermanence can in this way be heightened into a standpoint of momentariness, where something like a ‘framerate’ of consciousness starts to become apparent—a direction of practice which has been emphasised within some vipassana traditions. This is undoubtedly fascinating, not least because the deconstruction of the sense of duration that can happen at this level of detail (or so I’m told) has resonance with findings from contemporary cognitive neuroscience, which concern how the sense of a phenomenal ‘now’ is constructed from multiple distinct brain processes representing non-concurrent sensory data as occurring within a single window of simultaneity. That it might be possible in meditation to refine attention to such a degree that it cuts beneath the construction of the phenomenal now is nothing short of mind-blowing. Yet it is also reasonable to wonder how any of this may help to unbind clinging.

As a final thought I’ll briefly consider the implications of these insight frames for the way we may think about samādhi and metta. The idea that an impermanence way of looking leads naturally to microscopic attention pairs with a corresponding conception of samādhi as laser-like one-pointed focus. While such a state certainly can be viewed as one kind of samādhi, to identify it with samādhi would seem to create problems. For example it would mystify the relationship between samādhi and metta, because after all what does laser focus have to do with loving-kindness? If we reorient to an emptiness way of looking, however, we might instead conceive samādhi simply as the collectedness or unification of mind that we naturally rest into when we fabricate less. To use another of Rob Burbea’s useful phrases, samādhi is just the other face of letting go. One great advantage of this way of seeing things is that the relationship between samādhi and metta suddenly becomes clear. The cultivation of loving-kindness is simply a direct means of fabricating less self. In this way metta and samādhi emerge as two aspects of a single process, which the standpoint of emptiness reveals as the process of defabrication.

  1. Specifically, in Chapter 12. [1] ↩︎

item.title: The Three Turnings in Classical Yogacara
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: One of the key differences between the early Buddhist and later Mahayana teachings is the way they each portray the relationship of the path to its destination. The early teachings stress that in its natural state the mind is obscured by very real afflictions, and the path is accordingly presented as the effort to draw up a map of the afflicted mind and to then use it to systematically cut away the fetters at the root of suffering. Nirvana is achieved only once this active and strenuous process has been completed, a world-transcending state which marks a definitive exit from samsara.
item.content:encoded:
One of the key differences between the early Buddhist and later Mahayana teachings is the way they each portray the relationship of the path to its destination. The early teachings stress that in its natural state the mind is obscured by very real afflictions, and the path is accordingly presented as the effort to draw up a map of the afflicted mind and to then use it to systematically cut away the fetters at the root of suffering. Nirvana is achieved only once this active and strenuous process has been completed, a world-transcending state which marks a definitive exit from samsara.

In contrast, Mahayana traditions tend to stress that the mind is always already awakened, and construe the path as a process of coming to realise this. They are suspicious of modes of practice which treat the afflictions as substantially real, worrying that in doing to so they only reinforce the reifying tendency which inhibits realisation of the Buddha-nature which is already there. Instead they recommend practices based on not-doing, typically non-dual or open awareness practices which aim at a certain ‘abiding in thusness’ without conceptualisation (tantric practices depart from this in some ways, but inhabit the same overall framework). Samsara already is nirvana—your job is to notice that.

The Buddha’s own comments can be ambiguous on this point, as for example when he likens the path to the purification of gold. On the one hand the gold is already there. But on the other hand so are the impurities, and their removal implies a process of real change. How different are these two presentations, really? The question feels live for me, as I currently seem to be converging on a mode of practice which is essentially neo-Theravada insight meditation, but with a view of the Dharma which is informed primarily by Mahayana philosophy. On the one hand I find the detailed and systematic approach of insight meditation generates enthusiasm and curiosity in me in way that non-dual practices simply do not, at least at this stage of my practice. Even if you accept that the aim is to stop doing something, rather than to do something, you still need to have a grasp on what exactly it is that you’re aiming to stop doing. Or to use another of the Buddha’s similes, if the Dharma is likened to a raft then you can accept that the raft is just a tool for getting to the other shore, not an end in itself, while also acknowledging that you do in fact need a raft and can’t just teleport across the river.

Yet at the same time I remain sceptical of world-transcending interpretations of nirvana, and do agree that this model generates meta-ethical problems that can’t be resolved without embracing a more thorough-going philosophy of emptiness and something like the Bodhisattva ideal (though I’d perhaps add the caveat that exactly how these innovations solve those problems is far less obvious than generally acknowledged).

Squaring this circle has been one of the main factors driving me to explore Yogacara more deeply. Following on from Ben Connelley’s book on Vasubandhu’s Thirty Verses, which I discussed previously, I’ve been reading William Waldron’s superb Making Sense of Mind-Only [1], which is significantly more comprehensive and historical in scope. Like Connelly, Waldron rejects the interpretation of classical Yogacara (which includes the Yogacara sutras along with the classic expositions of Vasubandhu and Asanga in the 4th or 5th century) as a metaphysical idealism. Instead he sees it as a form of cognitive constructivism, more concerned with how experience arises as a function of the interaction between our environment and our mental faculties than with fundamental ontology.

Of particular interest here is the Yogacara teaching on the “three turnings of the wheel of Dharma,” a framework through which Yogacara synthesises the detailed psychological maps of earlier Buddhism with the new Mahayana emptiness teachings found in the Perfection of Wisdom sutras and their explication by Nagarjuna. The first turning refers to both the early Buddhist teachings as presented in the Pali discourses, and their systematisation in the various Abhidharma schools that followed.

According to the Abhidharma analysis all experience can be broken down into constitutive elements called (somewhat confusingly) dharmas, point-like moments of awareness constantly flashing in and out of existence. All the normal objects of our experience—medium-sized dry goods, thoughts, sensations, our sense of self—are just higher-level patternings of these momentary dharmas, appearing with a false solidity in roughly the same way that moving objects appear on a film screen despite being constituted by a rapid sequence of static frames. One of the features of many Abhidharma systems is that the dharmas are the only entities considered to ultimately exist, while everything else exists only in some derivative sense.

The second turning refers to the Mahayana emptiness teachings, and its distinctive innovation is that it rejects the ultimate reality of dharmas. A consequence of this stance is that it flattens the difference in status between the dharmas and aggregate phenomena, which are now all viewed as equally empty of essence or substantial reality. One lesson that could be taken from this, which later comes to define the Madhyamaka school, is that Buddhist theorists should stop trying to construct positive accounts of the functioning of the mind and reorient themselves toward the deconstruction of any and all views that treat—explicitly or implicitly—some entity or class of entities as non-empty.

While Madhyamaka could be described as an anti-metaphysics, it remains in a metaphysical register in the sense that its purely negative style of reasoning aims to deconstruct the fallacy of reification that underlies all positive metaphysical views, a fallacy it takes to be the primary barrier to liberation. In light of this it is perhaps not surprising that Madhyamaka philosophers like Candrakirti, writing several hundred years after Asanga and Vasubandhu, often construed Yogacara as a metaphysical idealism only to then accuse it of reifying the mind.

Waldron argues that Yogacara is better seen as a different kind of response to the emptiness teachings, one which can first be approached by considering the limitations of Madhyamaka. In restricting itself to purely deconstructive discourse, Madhyamaka deprives itself of the resources needed to answer certain kinds of questions. For example, Madhyamaka agrees that there is such a thing as conventional truth and hence that there is a difference between conventional truth and conventional falsehood. But at the same time it seems to preclude the possiblity of our ever being able to say what this difference consists in. The Madhyamika seems committed to a kind of quietism about the nature of conventional truth—the best they could say is that if you want to know what mathematical truth is, ask a mathematician; if you want to know what historical truth is, ask a historian; but there is no way of getting at an overarching, domain-neutral theory of conventional truth which the univocity of the concept seems to demand. This is not inconsistent, but it is certainly unsatisfying. The Madhyamika might say that the difference is just that some conventional beliefs are useful while others are not, but a statement like that remains vacuous in the absence of an account of what it is that might make a conventional belief useful in general. And it is exactly that kind of account that Madhyamaka forbids itself from offering.

Furthermore, from the point of view of liberation the issue is not really that we believe phenomena to non-empty. The issue is that we perceive them to be so, and this may be the case regardless of what we believe. If we are to free ourselves of our ingrained perception of the world as infused with illusory essence, what we’d really like is a substantial account of why it is that our mental faculties automatically impute essences to phenomena which do not in fact possess them. Once again, here Madhyamaka can only maintain a principled silence. But for Yogacara this is the key question, and what allows it to take it up as a positive program is precisely its reframing of the emptiness teachings in cognitive rather than metaphysical terms. This is the move which defines the third turning of the wheel of Dharma.

For Yogacarins, then, the relationship to the Abhidharma looks like this. As per the emptiness teachings they reject the ultimate reality of the dharmas, but they do not take this as reason to reject the Abhidharma framework as a whole. Instead they reinterpret the dharmas as something more like epistemological primitives than ontological primitives—as building blocks of a theory of mind rather than building blocks of reality. This allows them to use those aspects of the Abhidharma which successfully explain our mental patterns while augmenting them with extra elements which could help to explain those aspects of mind which the Abhidharma system struggled to accommodate. For instance in another of its signature teachings, Yogacara posits an unconscious layer of mental processing to account for memory and karmic continuity, neither of which can easily be made sense of in a system in which the only components are momentary, sequential, and manifestly present dharmas.

What all this points to is a way of integrating the Mahayana emptiness teachings with a form of practice that is continuous with, rather than divergent from, insight practices rooted in the substantial content of the first turning psychology. Without seeing these as descriptions of substantially real afflictions one can nevertheless actively engage them as necessary tools—as in the simile of the raft—in coming to see exactly how the illusion of substantiality is staged, and which can be discarded once they have served their purpose.

item.title: The Weird Materialism of Early Buddhism
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: A recurring theme in the Pali discourses is the so-called ‘reflections on the repulsiveness of the body,’ a set of practices whose prominence in the scriptures is difficult to square with modern sensibilities. A hefty chunk of the Satipatthana Sutta is devoted to these reflections, including a section on ‘cemetery contemplations’ in which the meditator is instructed to visualize the decomposition of their own corpse in gruesome detail. Similar examples are found elsewhere, such as this representative passage from the Kayagata-sati Sutta:
item.content:encoded:
A recurring theme in the Pali discourses is the so-called ‘reflections on the repulsiveness of the body,’ a set of practices whose prominence in the scriptures is difficult to square with modern sensibilities. A hefty chunk of the Satipatthana Sutta is devoted to these reflections, including a section on ‘cemetery contemplations’ in which the meditator is instructed to visualize the decomposition of their own corpse in gruesome detail. Similar examples are found elsewhere, such as this representative passage from the Kayagata-sati Sutta:

Furthermore, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, tendons, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, large intestines, small intestines, gorge, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, skin-oil, saliva, mucus, fluid in the joints, urine.’ Just as if a sack with openings at both ends were full of various kinds of grain — wheat, rice, mung beans, kidney beans, sesame seeds, husked rice — and a man with good eyesight, pouring it out, were to reflect, ‘This is wheat. This is rice. These are mung beans. These are kidney beans. These are sesame seeds. This is husked rice’; in the same way, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things.

Not your average body scan. Sometimes modern dharma teachers present these as marginal practices intended only to address hindrances involving lust, erotic fantasy, or vanity. But this doesn’t really account for the prevalence of passages like these, which seem to indicate something far more central to mindfulness of the body, appearing more or less anywhere it is under consideration. A better explanation is that they reflect a tendency in early Buddhism to view the sensual world as something to be transcended in its entirety, in contrast with modern framings which stress how mindfulness can both moderate and enhance engagement with sensual pleasures. Yet this is still rather general and nebulous, and one wonders whether the sheer oddness of these passages isn’t concealing some deeper and more specific lesson.

What I’d like to think about is how this preoccupation with gore points to the function of materiality—or rather a certain encounter with materiality—in the process of insight. I can begin to unpack what I mean by that by turning to the familiar example of pain. During a sit an aversive reaction to pain may be skillfully handled by bringing a calm attention to the moment-to-moment sensory content in the painful area. Doing so reveals the pain to be something much less substantial than it at first seems to be: a flux of physical micro-sensations (of heat, pressure, etc), not unpleasant in themselves but which seem to float in an unpleasant atmosphere which is distinct from them. Something peculiar happens with this shift in perspective, a ‘defabrication’ effect (to use Rob Burbea’s helpful term) which exposes the pain as a conceptual imputation, an empty phenomenon no longer solid enough for the aversion to get a grip on. In this way the defabrication of pain into its components—the (material) micro-sensations and (mental) feeling tone around them—brings about a loosening of dukkha in the associated sensory region.

What’s significant here is that the pain is first naively encountered as a simple bodily sensation, but defabrication shows that this “material” experience was always constructed through mental imputation at the same time as revealing its true material content, which is then encountered as something unsuspected and strange. This is why this kind of defabrication is sometimes likened to the experience of repeating a word until it loses all meaning, the sound of it suddenly seeming naked and strange in the absence of interpretation which is usually automatic and unconscious.

This strangeness may not be so apparent in the case of pain, but it becomes vivid in the defabrication of more pleasurable sensations. When first experimenting with mindful eating it is common to experience an increase in pleasure, perhaps even a dramatic increase. But turning up the granularity of attention and tuning into the moment-to-moment sensations lying beneath this pleasure can produce a similar effect to that described above in the case of pain—the aura of pleasantness separates out from the physical mechanics of chewing, the erratic movements of the tongue, the soggy matter being transferred from place to place in the mouth, all of which presents all of a sudden with a raw materiality that feels alien and kind of disgusting. The effect is not dissimilar to watching some creaky surrealist stop animation, like a film of Jan Svankmajer, a certain uncanny fascination. There’s a skeleton inside you and that creep is eating.

Insight experiences like these hint at what the reflections on the repulsiveness of the body may be alluding to. In naive or deluded consciousness what appears as a simple bodily sensation is in fact always a fabrication based on the intermingling of two distinct factors: materiality and mental imputation (rupa and nama). What the untangling of these two factors in insight experience provokes is an encounter with the material factor in its pure materiality, which is to say in the form of a radical otherness. The significance of repulsiveness, on this reading, is not so much to indicate a negative valuation of the body and its sensuality as that it encourages an encounter with the body in its fundamental otherness. This is not just otherness in the negative sense of being not-me, but a positive otherness which actively punctures and disrupts the field of conceptual imputation.

To look at it this way is to find in these early Buddhist practices a certain latent materialism. I use the word ‘materialism’ here because it is concerned with the inescapability of otherness in our contact with reality. Once we get past the substantialist tendency to categorise metaphysical positions on the basis of what kind of stuff they think reality is made of, the difference between materialism and idealism shows up ultimately as a disagreement about the status of otherness. The idealist idea that mind is fundamental to reality amounts to a promise that reality is something which is in an important sense already familiar to us; its task is then to account for the appearance of illusory otherness as an effect of dissociation, or the estrangement of consciousness from itself. But for the materialist otherness is irreducible and non-illusory, a basic facet of reality. Matter is just the name we give to this otherness, which manifests in the first place as the resistance reality puts up to our plans, to our theories, and to our unconscious mental imputations. Matter is the aspect of reality which pushes back.

Clearly, to read this kind of view back into early Buddhism is anachronistic at best, and I’m not claiming that early Buddhists were metaphysical materialists. The point of this is just to suggest that reading the tradition through the lens of materialism—something which in my experience many modern Buddhists in the West are extremely hostile to—can help to extract surprising lessons from parts of the Dharma that may otherwise remain puzzling.

item.title: Yogacara's Idealism
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: I’ve just finished Ben Connelly’s book Inside Vasubandhu’s Yogacara [1], which while primarily a practice guide also doubles as a rather good primer on Yogacara philosophy. Yogacara can feel particularly opaque, and understanding which aspects are psychological and which are more philosophical, what those philosophical aspects are trying to say, which bits are minor modifications of the Abhidharma framework and which are genuine innovations, if or how it disagrees with other Buddhist philosophical schools, and so on, can be tricky to say the least. But for the purposes this post I’m going to put all that aside and focus on the key Yogacara doctrine of ‘mind-only’.
item.content:encoded:
I’ve just finished Ben Connelly’s book Inside Vasubandhu’s Yogacara [1], which while primarily a practice guide also doubles as a rather good primer on Yogacara philosophy. Yogacara can feel particularly opaque, and understanding which aspects are psychological and which are more philosophical, what those philosophical aspects are trying to say, which bits are minor modifications of the Abhidharma framework and which are genuine innovations, if or how it disagrees with other Buddhist philosophical schools, and so on, can be tricky to say the least. But for the purposes this post I’m going to put all that aside and focus on the key Yogacara doctrine of ‘mind-only’.

Mind-only has precedents in the early Buddhist texts—most famously in the Dhammapada, which opens with the words “Mind precedes all mental states. Mind is their chief; they are all mind-wrought.”—but its development in and centrality to Yogacara has led it to be described as a Buddhist idealism, and much of the discussion around Yogacara aims to clarify what kind of idealism it is. One prominent current construes Yogacara as a kind of metaphysical idealism, claiming that mental states are all that ultimately exist. Going against this current, Connelly gives an epistemological reading of the mind-only doctrine as saying that nothing in our experience escapes conceptual mediation (a claim which is non-committal with regard to how things actually are).

To make a comparison with Western forms of idealism, Connelly’s reading places Yogacara much closer to the transcendental idealism of Kant than to the subjective idealism of Berkeley. And if this comparison of Yogacara with Kant is even halfway right, it should warn us that it will likely generate all sorts of confusion. One of the worries often leveled against epistemological idealism is that it fails to define a substantial position distinct from metaphysical idealism, i.e. that it either says something too trivial to be substantial, or that insofar as it says something substantial it does so only by smuggling in metaphysical commitments. This in mind, it’s worth unpacking some of the implications to get a better grasp on what it’s actually saying.

In early modern European philosophy it was common to make a distinction between perceptible properties—like colour and smell—which are mediated (or conditioned) by our perceptual systems, and those—like shape, position in space and time, unity, etc—which belong to the nature of the object itself, irrespective of the mediating contribution of our own mind. Kant rejected this picture by arguing that there are no perceptible properties which are not mediated by our own mental faculties, including resolutely ‘objective’ properties like shape, time, and space. Many have since wondered how metaphysical this kind of view is. If space and time are transcendentally ideal in the sense that they represent the conceptual activity of our mind in structuring experience, does this imply that what cosmologists are studying is really just the contents of our own minds? If so, this really would seem to commit Kant to a kind of metaphysical idealism, and a somewhat implausible one at that.

But there’s another way of reading Kant. On this reading, which deflates the metaphysical scope of his idealism, the fact that nothing in our experience escapes conceptual mediation is just a banal consequence of the fact that concepts are how we get a grip on the world. If an elephant experiences the world by grasping it with its trunk, then it is correct to say that everything in its experience is mediated by the contributing structure of its trunk. But it would be wrong to then conclude from this that it does not grasp the real world at all. And it is the same, the transcendental idealist says, with the way our experience is structured by our faculty of conceptual understanding.

This is the kind of interpretation which is sometimes accused of triviality, but it has radical implications. What it amounts to is a wholesale rejection of a certain model of cognition which in the West is associated most strongly with Descartes. According to this Cartesian model our experience of the world is mediated by mental representations, which taken together form the inner world of consciousness. We stand in an unmediated relation with the mental representations themselves, and then there is a secondary matter of how accurately these representations on the screen of consciousness mirror the real world. The direct objects of our experience are mental representations, and our experience of these is unmediated. In this sense these primary experiences are infallible—you can’t be wrong about the way things seem to you. The mediation (and hence fallibility) is only introduced at the second stage. Kant rejects this two-stage picture, instead taking the view that when you experience a tree, say, you are not having an unmediated experience of a mental representation of a tree (which may or may not accurately represent a real tree). You are simply having a conceptually mediated experience of the tree.

If this distinction feels obscure, it can be illustrated with a simple example that will also help bring this back to Yogacara. Say you’re walking home at night and are startled by what seems to be a person lurking at the side of the road, but on second glance you realise it is a tree swaying in the wind. The Kantian has a very simple story about what has happened here—they say that you saw a tree and misapplied the concept ‘person’. But for the Cartesian a question arises about what mental representation was present when the erroneous judgement was made. If they say that there was a mental representation of a tree which was misinterpreted as a person, then this violates the idea that we have unmediated access to our conscious experience. But if they say that there was a mental representation of a person, as it seems they will have to, then this opens the door to a generalised scepticism. Because if such a disjunction between mental representation and object represented is possible, then how can we know our mental representations ever accurately represent the world at all?

The Yogacarin’s picture looks very similar to the Kantian’s. They would say both that your experience of the real tree is mediated by karmic impressions residing in the ‘store consciousness’1 (in this case the combination of evolutionary, cultural, and perhaps personal conditioning which sets you up to see an unexpected tree as a menacing figure), and that there’s no way of stripping away this mediation to get at the raw or ‘true’ experience. This is true both in the case of erroneous experience as in the example, and in normal veridical experience. Indeed, mind-only is the idea that karmic mediation is constitutive of our conscious experience in general.

Put this way the position I am attributing to Kant and the Yogacarin may seem reasonable, but the Cartesian intuition it runs counter to—that there is some intrinsic way that things seem to us prior to our mind’s conceptual framing of those things—is hard to shake. We tend to think there is some way a particular shade of grey looks, and are surprised by optical illusions in which the same shade of grey appears different depending on its surrounding shades. We struggle to get our heads round the fact that a C major scale and an A minor scale—two quite different tonalities—contain exactly the same notes. We believe that we can see colour in our peripheral vision, but under experimental conditions perfectly well-sighted humans prove useless at distinguishing even vivid colours at the edge of their visual field. Afflictions like Anton syndrome—in which a person who is anatomically blind, who has no activity in their optical nerve and will bump into obstacles and fail to catch a ball when thrown at them, will nevertheless insist that they can see—trouble us at a philosophical level. We want to know what it is these people are actually experiencing, yet it seems hard to say what that might be or even what this question might mean.

The mind-only view that nothing in experience escapes conceptual mediation also has implications for how we think about insight experiences and awakening. As intimated earlier, if there is no unmediated experience then all experience is fallible. In particular there is no special category of ‘direct’ experience (of emptiness, thusness, Buddha-nature, etc) that is intrinsically truth-revealing.

In the Thirty Verses on Mind-Only—the classic Yogacara text by 4th/5th Century Buddhist philosopher Vasubandhu that Connelly’s book is based on—there’s a striking example of this when Vasubandhu asserts that the other-dependent nature of a phenomenon, which is its ultimate truth as empty or dependently arisen, is itself a conceptualisation. This is an idea which goes against the grain of many framings of awakening, particularly those which lean on a metaphysical interpretation of mind-only. Among these it’s not uncommon to find the following story: consciousness is fundamental to reality, and conceptualisation is an extra layer added by our minds to the raw experiential flux, including the conceptualisation that separates reality into self and other. On this view awakening is about lifting this veil of conceptualisation to come into a direct (i.e. unmediated) contact with pure, unconceptualised consciousness, which is also ultimate reality. This is precisely the kind of model rejected by Connelly’s Vasubandhu. For Yogacara so understood, awakening is not about going beyond conceptual mediation but rather ‘resting in mind-only’—the recognition of the conceptually mediated nature of experienced reality as conceptually mediated, together with the recognition that there is nothing beyond mediation.

  1. The store consciousness (alaya-vijnana) is another key Yogacara concept, describing an unconscious layer of the mind where karmic impressions are ‘stored’. ↩︎

item.title: The Logic of the Middle Way
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: In its original form, the Middle Way referred to a spiritual orientation that charts a path between the twin extremes of egoism and asceticism. But in its broader and more philosophical sense the Middle Way is usually defined in relation to the Scylla and Charybdis of nihilism and eternalism. These are not so much philosophical positions as common forms of answer which may be given in response to many different philosophical questions. Egoism and asceticism, for instance, correspond to nihilist and eternalist conceptions of the self. But equally there are nihilist and eternalist positions on morality, external reality, causality, knowledge, and so on.
item.content:encoded:
In its original form, the Middle Way referred to a spiritual orientation that charts a path between the twin extremes of egoism and asceticism. But in its broader and more philosophical sense the Middle Way is usually defined in relation to the Scylla and Charybdis of nihilism and eternalism. These are not so much philosophical positions as common forms of answer which may be given in response to many different philosophical questions. Egoism and asceticism, for instance, correspond to nihilist and eternalist conceptions of the self. But equally there are nihilist and eternalist positions on morality, external reality, causality, knowledge, and so on.

But nihilism and eternalism are not quite the same kind of position, and are perhaps better seen as orthogonal to one another. Eternalism concerns the ontological status of something—to be an eternalist about mathematics is to hold that mathematical objects are substantially real and mind-independent (mathematical Platonism, for example, is a strong form of eternalism); to be an eternalist about morality is hold that there is a moral reality that exists prior to and independently of human attitudes.
