Nihilism, on the other hand, concerns our relationship to something, which is usually conceived in epistemic or normative terms. Scepticism is a form of epistemological nihilism—it says our claim to knowledge of the external world can never be justified. Moral nihilism says that we can never have reason to be moral, or to put it differently, that there is no basis for morality’s authority, for the normative grip it is supposed to have on us.

In this respect, nihilism and eternalism are logically independent of one another—the question of how I can be justified in my beliefs about the external world is separate from the question of the ontological status of that external world. But historically the two have been closely linked, in both East and West, in the specific sense that eternalist positions have often been adopted as a means of avoiding nihilistic conclusions. So we might think that in order to secure the authority of morality we have to first secure the objectivity of moral talk, and to do that we have to posit a mind-independent moral reality which such talk describes. Fundamentally, the Middle Way (in its philosophical guise) is a rejection of this whole idea that nihilistic conclusions can be avoided by adopting eternalist positions.

In fact it’s a bit stronger than that—what the Middle Way asserts is not just that eternalism fails to block nihilism, but that eternalism actually entails nihilism. If true then this implies that avoiding the nihilistic conclusion requires an explicit rejection of eternalism. A vivid example of this pattern of reasoning is provided by Nāgārjuna, who at the beginning of his Fundamental Verses applies it to causality [1]. The standard argument might be that in order to make sense of the presence of the causal relation in our experience of reality, we have to treat this reality as composed of self-subsisting discrete entities which can stand to one another in relations of cause and effect (a kind of eternalism about objects). Nāgārjuna turns this on its head to argue that if reality is made up of entities which really are discrete in this sense, then we cannot possibly account of the presence of the causal relation. If we are to avoid the nihilistic conclusion that causality is illusory or unknowable, then we must reject an eternalist interpretation of the relata of the causal relation.

Similar examples are found in modern Western philosophy. Mathematical Platonism has often been seen as an attractive position because it seems able to account for the peculiarly robust nature of mathematical truth. But it has also been pointed out that if mathematical objects really do exist in the sense required by Platonism then they will have to be very strange objects indeed, immaterial and unchanging at the least. But if so then it is unclear how they could possibly stand in causal relations with our own cognitive faculties, in which case how could we ever know anything about them? The very move that is made to secure the absolute quality of mathematical knowledge ultimately threatens to uncut its status even as regular knowledge. To argue that mathematical Platonism must be false if mathematical knowledge is to be possible is akin to Middle Way reasoning.

Same with morals. A belief in mind-independent moral reality is often assumed to be necessary to avert moral nihilism, but there’s a distinctively modern line of thought which argues that moral realism (as this form of eternalism is known) produces a problem of normative alienation, which ultimately undercuts the force of moral reasons. If ‘good’ and ‘bad’ are real properties of actions in roughly the same sense that positive and negative charge are real properties of particles, then how can we possibly make sense of the authority moral judgement is supposed to exert over our decision making? The various lines of thought which flow from this—clustered under headings like ‘metaethical expressivism’ and ‘Kantian constructivism’ and ‘quasi realism’—all share the basic idea that morality exerts authority over us only because it is an institution we create, not a reality we discover. The worry that this will undermine the objectivity of morality and reduce it to mere preference will have to be answered in some other way, and indeed much energy in contemporary metaethics is directed into this project.

In summary, the core point here is that the Middle Way is not simply a third option added to the pair of nihilism and eternalism, but is better viewed as a stance on the relationship between them. This stance, which inverts the traditional assumption that eternalism is required to avert nihilism by arguing that eternalism entails nihilism, and therefore must in fact be rejected to avert it, is epitomised by Nāgārjuna’s analysis of causality and other phenomena. But it is also embodied by the quasi-realist project in modern Western philosophy, which aims to reject nihilism in various spheres of discourse by rejecting strong forms of realism about their objects.

item.title: Impermanence, Impermanence, Impermanence
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: While all of the three marks of existence present their own conceptual difficulties, anicca (impermanence) has always seemed to me the most slippery, particularly where insight is concern. This is not because it is harder to comprehend than dukkha or anattā (unsatisfactoriness or no-self), but for the opposite reason that it is almost too obvious. We already know that relationships end, that we will die some day, that whims come and go, that tectonic plates drift and that a clay pot left in the ground for a few thousand years will crumble and decay. We also know that if we watch something long enough we will see it change. What, then, can insight into impermanence really consist in?
item.content:encoded:
While all of the three marks of existence present their own conceptual difficulties, anicca (impermanence) has always seemed to me the most slippery, particularly where insight is concern. This is not because it is harder to comprehend than dukkha or anattā (unsatisfactoriness or no-self), but for the opposite reason that it is almost too obvious. We already know that relationships end, that we will die some day, that whims come and go, that tectonic plates drift and that a clay pot left in the ground for a few thousand years will crumble and decay. We also know that if we watch something long enough we will see it change. What, then, can insight into impermanence really consist in?

One thought is that insight into impermanence is not so much about observing elements of experience as impermanent, but about observing the way that our mind imbues the objects of experience with artificial permanence. Insight will thus consist, at least in part, of the recognition of a gap between the way we intuitively treat things and the way we know them to be. It could be argued that those of us born into the post-scientific world with our knowledge of thermodynamics, evolution by natural selection and cosmic expansion, are better placed to grasp impermanence than anyone has ever been. But this would just mean that the persistence of those default attitudes, which represent the world as infused with false permanence, should be all the more surprising—an even more fertile ground for insight.

Everyday cravings and aversions, for instance, seem to involve a perception of at least relative permanence. The form of attachment distinctive of craving manifests as a certain ‘solidification’ of desire, a projection of temporal persistent. A desire perceived as temporally persistent is one that demands to be satisfied, in that it may seem as if the only way to ease the discomfort accompanying an unsatisfied desire is by acquiring its object. That this is a projection of permanence is reflected in the fact that it gains force only by masking another means of easing of discomfort, namely by waiting out the desire. Left alone a desire can be observed to play out by itself and eventually to fade away. By directly noticing the impermanence of desire in this way, insight can filter into intuition and loosen the grip of craving.

The principle at work here is that impermanence is essentially self-revealing, in the sense that the only thing required to intuitively grasp something’s impermanence is to consistently bring attention to it. The converse of this point is that those things we take (falsely) to be permanent will be those we do not tend to bring attention to. Those things we technically know to be impermanent but which remain out of mind could be said to form the intuitively ‘stable’ background of life, against which the objects of our immediate attention dart and flicker. This picture goes some way to explaining why disruptions to that background, events which may seem inevitable in retrospect—the death of a close relative, a sudden deterioration of health—can seem at the time like an intrusion of the unthinkable. This points to a second, perhaps deeper form that insight into impermanence can take: the direct noticing of background elements as belonging to the background. Such a noticing implies the movement of an element from the background into the foreground of awareness, which will ensure its stability is revealed to intuition (not just to knowledge) as illusory.

Pushing this idea to its logical conclusion yields a third level of insight, concerning the universal scope of impermanence. Having engaged in this practice persistently, bringing attention to background elements which are thereby revealed to intuition as impermanent, we can imagine that we might begin to gain an intuitive sense of the impermanence not just of particular things, but of every thing. This has implications for the way we perceive change in general, because it is part of our natural way of seeing things that change is perceived as something that happens only relative to a fixed background. But what the universal scope of impermanence implies is that there is no fixed background, only impermanent phenomena unfolding at different rates.

A vivid example of how this might affect our intuitive experience is provided by an argument that originates with Aristotle, and that was an important source of resistance to the Copernican model of the solar system in the 1500s. This was the so-called ‘tower argument,’ which was taken for a long time to definitively uphold the Ptolemaic model according to which celestial objects revolve around a stationary Earth. This argument pointed to the fact that a rock dropped 1 meter from the top of a tower falls directly to the ground and lands 1 meter from its base. If the Earth were in motion, so the reasoning went, then the tower would have moved by the time the rock landed, and it would hit the ground at a different distance from its base.

This reasoning is now recognised as fallacious, and one crucial aspect of Galileo’s campaign to promote Copernicanism was his illustration of what goes wrong with it. He realised that the tower argument depends on the assumption that motion is absolute, which is to say all motion occurs relative to a universal frame of reference (an ultimate fixed background). Underwritten by this implicit principle is the assumption that except in clear-cut instances of illusion, apparent motion coincides with real motion (and therefore that apparent stability coincides with real stability). In developing what is now known as Galilean relativity—in which all motion is conceived as relative and there is no universal frame of reference—Galileo in effect undermined this assumption, and with it the intuitive force of the tower argument.1

The fundamental point here is a counter-intuitive one: that once the idea that change is always change relative to a fixed background has been rejected—once we appreciate that our perception of change is only ever perception of change relative to things which are themselves changing, i.e. that impermanence is a true universal—we should expect the illusion of stability to be ubiquitous. While the analogy is somewhat impressionistic, in this example of Galilean relativity and the tower argument we catch a glimpse of how knowledge of impermanence, which in the first place may seem obvious, can ultimately yield insight into the fundamental nature of how reality is constructed in experience, so long as it is realised at the level of intuition and applied universally.

  1. A wonderful philosophical account of the conceptual shifts involved in the Copernican revolution can be found in Paul Feyerabend’s classic Against Method [1]. ↩︎

item.title: Anattā and the Screen of Consciousness
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: In this post I’d like to briefly consider a metaphor that comes up often in relation to consciousness, in many different contexts. Anyone who follows contemporary philosophy of mind will know how important the idea of a ‘screen of consciousness’ is for framing the big questions. It is often used to illustrate the hard problem of consciousness, for example, as it is in David Chalmer’s popular introduction where he asks us to consider the ‘inner cinema’ of conscious experience. There’s also a long line of physicalist thinkers from Richard Rorty to Daniel Dennett who have tried to deconstruct it, arguing that the screen is a bad metaphor generating false problems. Here I’ll consider it from the point of view of meditative experience and awakening, where I believe similar tensions are in play.
item.content:encoded:
In this post I’d like to briefly consider a metaphor that comes up often in relation to consciousness, in many different contexts. Anyone who follows contemporary philosophy of mind will know how important the idea of a ‘screen of consciousness’ is for framing the big questions. It is often used to illustrate the hard problem of consciousness, for example, as it is in David Chalmer’s popular introduction where he asks us to consider the ‘inner cinema’ of conscious experience. There’s also a long line of physicalist thinkers from Richard Rorty to Daniel Dennett who have tried to deconstruct it, arguing that the screen is a bad metaphor generating false problems. Here I’ll consider it from the point of view of meditative experience and awakening, where I believe similar tensions are in play.

In the context of insight meditation, the screen of consciousness is sometimes used to describe the process of dis-identification from the various mental elements that constitute our experience of self. So for example, when a thought or desire arises in meditation we intend to notice it rather than identify with it—in doing so this mental formation moves from the ground of consciousness (where it would normally be pulling our strings) into the figure, where it appears as one more object on the screen of consciousness and where it can play out according to its own internal dynamics without our involvement.

When this reflex gets strong the effect can be quite dramatic. Once or twice I’ve had an experience where enough concentration, calm and equanimity have developed that arising elements start to automatically take on this objective, dis-identified quality, without any active noticing taking place. In these moments—which in my case have always been brief and unstable, just glimpses really—there’s a very strong sense of ‘zooming out’ as the entire field of consciousness becomes unified, each sensation, thought and volition appearing as one more brushstroke on the same canvas. In these moments consciousness really does seem screen-like, and the use of the metaphor to describe this process feels perfectly apt.

When I’ve discussed this experience with a teacher, they have said that what is being glimpsed in this sense of zooming out is the process of awakening itself, which involves precisely this disentangling of pure awareness from the constitutive elements of the self structure. But something about this explanation seems incomplete to me, at least insofar as awakening is concerned. The components of self are traditionally described by the five aggregates of clinging: form/matter (rūpa), feeling (vedanā), perception (saññā), mental formations (saṅkhārā), and awareness (viññāṇa). But in the zooming out process it seems to me that what is really happening is that dis-identification with the first four aggregates is being achieved only through a particularly strong kind of identification with the fifth.

The point I’m making here is just that as various aspects of the self move from the ground of consciousness into the figure, as awareness as such is disentangled from its identification with what it is aware of, the difference between figure and ground is not only left in tact but is actually reinforced. We can certainly imagine a limit point of this process, perhaps even accept that there is some threshold that once crossed ensures the zoomed-out-ness remains permanent. But is this really a good thing to aim for? What is left at this limit is a pure point of awareness—a subject without a self—peering out on a world no part of which it identifies with, and so in which it does not really participate. But this seems to me to be a subject which still clings to its sense of itself as a subject, and the objects of its awareness as things it is simply not involved with.

If we take seriously the idea that realising anattā involves letting go of the fifth aggregate—awareness itself—as well as the preceding four, then surely what will have to go is this whole imagery of a screen of consciousness. The effect of this metaphor is to license the idea of consciousness as constituting a separate, self-subsisting domain between the subject and world on the other side—an eye of the mind viewing the images on the screen of the inner cinema, which may or may not be accurate representations of the world outside. Logical problems aside, as far as Buddhism is concerned what’s important to note is that this picture treats consciousness as non-empty (it’s an underappreciated point about emptiness that it is supposed to apply as much to the inner as to the outer world). Whether we view it from the perspective of the five aggregates or through the philosophy of emptiness, the metaphor of the screen has to go.

What could it mean to let go of awareness itself? Well, perhaps the key thing here is that what must disappear is the very difference between the figure and ground of consciousness, and with it the sense of a dis-identified subject peering at an inner screen. What this gestures to is a radical inversion of the whole process, where the ‘zooming out’ turns into something more like a ‘plunging in.’ In the end the real lesson is perhaps not that it is possible to zoom all the way out, or that it is possible to do so permanently, but that the very possibility of zooming out shows that the figure/ground relationship is itself mutable and impermanent, and that the screen of consciousness is itself just another construct.

item.title: Emptiness and Dependent Designation
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
Whatever is dependently arisen, That is explained to be emptiness. That, being a dependent designation, Is itself the Middle Way.—Nāgārjuna1

Many people have tried to explain the concept of emptiness in many different ways, but among these two broad approaches can be discerned, each emphasising one facet of the three-way identity between emptiness, dependent arising, and dependent designation given by Nāgārjuna in his famous verse.

The first approach stresses the link between emptiness and dependent arising. According to this interpretation, emptiness is the idea that things lack any intrinsic reality over and above the extrinsic relationships they enter into with other things. These may be cause-effect relationships, part-whole relationships, or any other kind of ontological dependency. Universal emptiness is presented on this view as a kind of metaphysical structuralism in which relations have ontological priority over their relata. This picture has various implications—that reality cannot be resolved into atoms or building blocks, for instance—but in it there is no special role for the perceiving mind.
item.content:encoded:
Whatever is dependently arisen, That is explained to be emptiness. That, being a dependent designation, Is itself the Middle Way.—Nāgārjuna1

Many people have tried to explain the concept of emptiness in many different ways, but among these two broad approaches can be discerned, each emphasising one facet of the three-way identity between emptiness, dependent arising, and dependent designation given by Nāgārjuna in his famous verse.

The first approach stresses the link between emptiness and dependent arising. According to this interpretation, emptiness is the idea that things lack any intrinsic reality over and above the extrinsic relationships they enter into with other things. These may be cause-effect relationships, part-whole relationships, or any other kind of ontological dependency. Universal emptiness is presented on this view as a kind of metaphysical structuralism in which relations have ontological priority over their relata. This picture has various implications—that reality cannot be resolved into atoms or building blocks, for instance—but in it there is no special role for the perceiving mind.

In the second approach the perceiving mind is central, however, as what it highlights is the link between emptiness and dependent designation. On this interpretation, to say that a phenomenon is empty is to say that its existence is dependent on our conceptual designation of it. I recently heard this position expressed by Rob Burbea in a podcast, where he puts it by saying that things cannot exist independently of a ‘way of looking’. This view presents emptiness as a kind of metaphysical subjectivism, stressing the role played by our attitudes and conventions in producing the reality we inhabit.

While both approaches get at something important about emptiness, left as they are they also seem to miss something crucial. In Nāgārjuna’s verse emptiness is identified with both dependent arising and dependent designation, and presumably what we’d like from a theory of emptiness is an account of how it links them. But neither of the interpretations above do this—in fact each appears to problematise the other. If emptiness is understood as a kind of subjectivism, then how can we can account for the objective structure of conventional reality? The conceptual designations we use are up to us, and so what’s to prevent their being arbitrary and this view collapsing into relativism? On the other hand if emptiness is understood as structuralism then while the objectivity of conventional reality is not threatened, it casts no light on our position as subjects within that reality. On this view emptiness of self is just one instance of the emptiness of phenomena in general, and so it provides no particular insight into what it might mean to realise no-self, and how this might transform our relationship to reality and to other sentient beings.

There’s a lot to unpick here, but for now I just want to gesture to an idea I rarely see mentioned in relation to emptiness: social constructivism. It’s often said that emptiness offers a middle way between existence and non-existence, but less cryptically we might say it’s about making a distinction between two different kinds of reality, namely substantial reality and effective reality. Something has effective reality if it is causally active in the world—i.e. it participates in the web of dependent arising—whereas it has substantial reality if it exists “from its own side”, which is to say as a substantial entity which could in principle exist by itself. To say that something is empty but not unreal is to say, then, that it has effective reality without substantial reality. That there could be such things is perhaps counter-intuitive, as effective realities are generally taken to be underwritten by substantial realities, but logically this need not be the case. Social construction is useful to think about in this regard, as it clearly illustrates the way in which effective realities can float freely of the substantial realities which are supposed to ground them. Even more suggestively, it describes a mechanism by which an act of designation produces an effective reality by falsely representing it as a substantial reality.

An example that illustrates this is the divine right of kings. In medieval Europe the authority of the king was predicated on the belief that it was God-given. The collective belief in this substantial reality—that the king’s authority was a kind of natural property belonging to that particular man, independently of the attitudes of his subjects—is what produced the effective reality of that authority, i.e. the fact that people would generally do things when the king commanded it. The authority is in truth no more than its designation (the subjects’ belief in it) but because that designation represents it falsely as a substantial reality (a property of the man himself) the designations are coordinated among the group, and the authority is brought into being as a de facto (i.e. effective) reality.

In this way we can see that things belonging to social-normative categories—things like property, money, laws, titles, etc—are empty in an especially vivid kind of way. They are causally effective without substantial basis, as a direct result of our collective designations. Admittedly Nāgārjuna’s claim is that emptiness is universal, and therefore applies as much to natural kinds like trees and planets as to social kinds, and it is harder to see how this mechanism might apply in those cases. The self, however—the paradigm example of an empty phenomenon that we mistakenly take to be non-empty— is something we might view as a social kind. To view the self as empty because socially constructed is to view it as an effective reality which is produced by its false representation as a substantial reality. I’ll wrap up this post with a few loosely collected thoughts and questions that arise from this:

  1. If the self is socially constructed, then who is doing the constructing? There’s two possible answers to this, one top-down and one bottom-up. The top-down answer is that the individual self is a product of other people and their attitudes. This is reminiscent of Hegel’s model of mutual recognition, which holds that self-consciousness is realised in individuals only through their recognition of other conscious beings who recognise them as such in turn. The bottom-up answer is that it is the community of sub-selves—the individual processes of mind which may take the controls at any moment, may have conflicting interests and which we aim to unify in samadhi—that posit themselves as a single substantial entity. I don’t know how to make this point yet but my intuition is that the top-down and bottom-up accounts are convergent. There’s a question that often arises about the relation between morality and awakening in Buddhism: do we aim for awakening in order to bring about the end of suffering for all sentient beings, or do we cultivate morality in order to hasten our awakening? Neither answer is satisfactory, and it seems to me the only way out of this impasse is to find a really deep reason why these two goals are identical. This way of thinking about the self contains resources for this—it points to a profound structural identity between unification of the mind and moral concern for others.

  2. Buddhism tells us that we are hardwired with a delusion of substantial selfhood and that this is the root of suffering, but no-one ever seems to wonder why we might be afflicted with this delusion in the first place. The social construction account gives an answer to this unasked question: we take ourselves (wrongly) to be a substantial self because this is a way of successfully producing an effective self, a means of self-creation. So the delusion does indeed have a purpose, the issue is that this particular means of producing an effective self is fragile and comes with structural problems, namely craving and dukkha. This both helps to show why Buddhism really does dice with nihilism—by undermining the substantial self there is a threat that the effective self will go with it—and why the project of awakening is ultimately positive in its aim to produce a version of effective selfhood which is freed from the illusion of substantial selfhood, and therefore from craving and dukkha.

  3. There is a way this picture can make sense of the universal scope of emptiness, at the same time as recognising emptiness of self as a special case. Reificiation of self, it could be argued, is a sort of foundational reification that facilitates the rest. The false substantiality imputed to the subject has a corresponding effect in imputing an illusory substantiality to the phenomena that make up the objective world outside it. The reification of the objective world is, on this account, a consequence of the way that a reified self materialises subject-object duality for itself. It is not that trees and planets are socially constructed as such, but that their independent ‘thinghood’ is. A reified self necessarily locates itself within a world populated with objects it perceives as equally reified, as substantial and self-subsisting, masking their true nature as empty positions within the web of causal interrelations. Realising no-self, on this view, is exactly the same as realising universal emptiness.

  1. Mūlamadhyamakakārikā Chapter 24, verse 18. [1] ↩︎

item.title: Craving, Delusion, and Unattached Desire
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: Throughout its history Buddhism has had to fend off the charge that it is a life-denying or nihilistic philosophy. This view was certainly held by Brahmanical thinkers in ancient India, who were critical of Buddhism’s atheism and doctrine of no-self, but it has also been a regular feature of its reception in the West. Perhaps the most famous representative of this trend was Nietzsche, who wrote in The Antichrist that “[Buddhism] is nihilistic in the sense that it aims at nothingness: life is overcome by withdrawal, desire is extinguished, and Nirvana is the final goal.”1
item.content:encoded:
Throughout its history Buddhism has had to fend off the charge that it is a life-denying or nihilistic philosophy. This view was certainly held by Brahmanical thinkers in ancient India, who were critical of Buddhism’s atheism and doctrine of no-self, but it has also been a regular feature of its reception in the West. Perhaps the most famous representative of this trend was Nietzsche, who wrote in The Antichrist that “[Buddhism] is nihilistic in the sense that it aims at nothingness: life is overcome by withdrawal, desire is extinguished, and Nirvana is the final goal.”1

Modern Buddhists are often quick to dismiss Nietzsche’s criticism as a misunderstanding, pointing out that the thing Buddhism aims to liberate us from is what in the Pali is called taṇhā, which means craving rather than mere desire. A distinction is made between unwholesome desires to be extinguished and wholesome desires to be cultivated, and it is on this basis that Nirvana is distinguished from nothingness or withdrawal.

It may be tempting to leave it at that, but it seems to me that the picture is rather more complicated. Because it is also part of the Buddhist view that there is a fundamental delusion at the root of our experience of reality, and the effect of this delusion is that our worldy desires automatically take on the form of cravings. This perhaps explains why (despite protests to the contrary) it is not uncommon to find Nirvana described as a cessation of desire or feeling. It may be granted that the Pali word taṇhā does have a stronger connotation of craving than the English ‘desire’, but since the ubiquity of delusion ensures that craving is coextensive with desire, the two are often treated as synonyms in practice.

Translating taṇhā as desire rather than craving could even be seen as a way of emphasising the Buddhist message. If Buddhism just said that happiness is about freeing oneself from craving, then it wouldn’t be clear what it was adding to common sense. The substance of the Buddhist analysis lies in its further claim that the root of delusion makes craving an inherently difficult problem to solve—indeed one that cannot be solved by normal forms of self-development, but only through the attainment of insight.

The picture is further complicated by a tendency within Buddhist tradition to group wholesome and unwholesome desires according to their content: desire for spiritual liberation or for the well-being of others is considered wholesome, while desire relating to sense-pleasures tends to be regarded as unwholesome. This grouping can make Buddhism seem, if not exactly life-denying, then certainly conservative. But really the difference is not a matter of the content of a desire, but whether or not it has the form of attachment. Does this mean, then, that sensual desire can take on a wholesome form as well, or at least that there is a skilful way of relating to sense-pleasure that does not involve renunciation?

The issue is potentially quite complex. Certainly the point is not that enjoyment of sensual pleasure is unwholesome in itself, and the discovery that mindfulness can amplify the pleasure of an activity like eating is usually viewed to be a treasure of the practice. But things become murkier if we consider the intentions or actions we might take in the pursuit of sense pleasures. In the usual analysis desire first arises dependent on feeling, and in the next moment we identify with the desire, thus empowering it as action. This moment of identification is the link at which delusion is active, and at which we have an opportunity to break the chain of reactivity. But at the same time it is clear that we must empower some intentions or desires, otherwise we would never act at all. What seems to emerge from this is a model of reflective endorsement: when mindfulness is established a volition can arise in consciousness without our being immediately identified with it. From this position of reflective distance it becomes possible to pose the question of whether or not we endorse it as reason to act.

And the idea, presumably, is that we should not endorse those desires that take the form of attachment. The fundamental reason for this seems to be that these desires are self-undermining, in that they are structured in such a way as to foreclose the possibility of their ever being satisfied. This happens, it is claimed, through the imposition of the self structure on experience—both the subjective and the objective poles of experience are artificially solidified so that the desire is represented as belonging to a fixed self, and the object of desire is projected as a self-subsisting entity that lies behind the flux of passing sensations. What is pursued is not pleasure itself but something more like the concept of pleasure—the reified pleasure ideal that deluded consciousness imposes on the transitory, impermanent phenomena that constitute the actual experience of pleasure. This natural tendency of the mind sets up a dynamic in which the real experience of pleasure is constantly being passed over in pursuit of an ideal object of desire, one which is guaranteed to elude our grasp because it does not really exist.

The picture sketched above seems attractive to me, for three reasons. First, it situates the discussion within the actual dynamics of craving. It speaks to that category of experience in which we forget to enjoy the first drink because we’re already thinking about the second, in which the intensity of romantic infatuation destroys the possibility of genuine intimacy, or in which anxiety about outcomes undermines the relaxed confidence needed to bring them about. Second, it highlights the relevance of these teachings to specifically modern forms of alienation. Jean Baudrillard memorably described the essence of consumerist society as a “reduction of the real to its representation,” the cultural logic at the root of the familiar and all-pervasive junk food effect in which life becomes a constant process of oscillating between anticipation and disappointment, as the reality of the things we consume never quite live up to the ideal constructed in advertising and in our minds. Buddhism tells us that this tendency is built into consciousness at the most fundamental level, and that these kind of consumerist dynamics only work by latching onto and amplifying this pre-existing potential.

Third, it moves attention from the content of desire onto its form. It is perfectly possible to cling to the idea of liberation—this is why the whole idea of striving for awakening has proved so problematic within Buddhism, and has been such an important driver of its development over the centuries. But to recognise that spiritual or moral desires can have the form of attachment is also to recognise that our more worldy desires have the potential to take on an unattached form, and this points the way to something potentially very life-affirming. A desire liberated from attachment is a desire which can be fulfilled. This is the form desire takes when extricated from the delusional framework that takes the world as a collection of unchanging objects changing only in their relations to one another, rather than as a collection of ever-changing processes whose alignments and eddy currents present illusions of stability (anicca), that posits an equally stable self which stands in relations of possession or negation to these objects (anatta), and the impossibility of satisfaction necessitated by this scheme (dukkha).

  1. Quote appears in §20. [1] ↩︎

item.title: Preliminary Notes on Rebirth
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: Discussing the trouble aspiring practitioners may have with the notion of rebirth, Bhikku Bodhi once suggested that the Dharma can be initially approached without reference to past and future lives, and that openness to such ideas need develop only once one has come to trust the teachings as a whole. Trust of this sort is arrived at not by blind faith, but by applying the teachings and experiencing for oneself their efficacy in the here and now.1
item.content:encoded:
Discussing the trouble aspiring practitioners may have with the notion of rebirth, Bhikku Bodhi once suggested that the Dharma can be initially approached without reference to past and future lives, and that openness to such ideas need develop only once one has come to trust the teachings as a whole. Trust of this sort is arrived at not by blind faith, but by applying the teachings and experiencing for oneself their efficacy in the here and now.1

This is an undeniably neat package, one which resists the secularising tendency to dismiss rebirth as an add-on to the Buddhist worldview while preserving the spirit of empirical self-enquiry that distinguishes Buddhism from faith-based religions. It also appeals to a form of reasoning we already apply elsewhere. We do not place trust in science because we have verified each and every theory for ourselves; instead we find its practical success evidenced in numerous small ways around us every day. This generates confidence in the scientific community and its practices of knowledge production as a whole, whose items of consensus are then seen as reliable even where they concern topics outside the scope of everyday experience. There are some important differences—Bhikku Bodhi proposes the Dharma be verified through personal experience, whereas scientific verification involves intersubjective methods like replication and peer review—but the point that our acceptance of exotic truths is mediated by trust in the institutions or frameworks that successfully produce mundane ones is the same in both cases.

Still, it is important to this picture that rebirth plays an essential functional role within the edifice of the Dharma. If it did not, then a deepening trust in the Dharma would be compatible with a rejection of rebirth. What is this functional role? We know that rebirth refers to a moment in the process of cyclic existence, a process brought to an end in the attainment of nirvāṇa. But it is not always clear how this is supposed to link up with other teachings. None of it seems to be required to grasp the basic idea that suffering is caused by attachment to impermanent things, for example, or that it is our mental reactions to discomfort that often produce the most discomfort. On first glance rebirth may even seem to be in direct tension with another Buddhist doctrine: anātman, or no-self.

This final observation is often taken as the departure point for clarifying the meaning of rebirth. What rebirth is not, it is insisted, is reincarnation—the Hindu conception in which an eternal and unchanging self is transferred from one material body to the next, something ruled out explicitly by the no-self doctrine. Buddhist rebirth is instead understood on the model of causal continuity, with the metaphor often invoked that of a flame passing from one candle to another. The flame is just an unfolding causal process absent any unitary underlying substance, yet it possesses a certain cohesion as a process, one which is preserved as it is transferred between candles.

But what this leaves us with is a picture of rebirth that is, metaphysical technicalities aside, equivalent to reincarnation in practice. And it seems to me there’s a few reasons to be dissatisfied with this. One is the old matter of whether we have good evidence for it. If scientific naturalism gives us reason to doubt reincarnation, then it will do the same for rebirth if understood this way. One could perhaps resist this conclusion by maintaining that the causal continuity in question happens in the realm of consciousness only, independently of any physical happenings where it might show up empirically. But to make this argument is to commit oneself to a kind of psychophysical dualism, something I take it the Buddhist does not want to do.

I won’t dwell on this objection, as I believe there’s a more fundamental and more interesting problem with the flame analogy. If rebirth is interpreted this way then it is being construed as something that just happens, a natural process belonging to the general order of things—like the rising of the sun or the changing of seasons—whether or not we believe ‘natural’ amounts to ‘physical’, and whether or not we believe science has the final say in determining how the natural world operates. But if this is the case then the relationship between rebirth and the more psychological facets of the Dharma will be left obscure. If it is something that just happens, then how could delusion or unwholesome desire play a causal role in perpetuating the cycle of rebirths? How could the cultivation of an awakening factor like equanimity help facilitate an exit from this cycle? How would rebirth then differ fundamentally from another natural process like bodily death, which is not prevented by the attainment of nirvāṇa?

If we are to have answers to these questions, then it seems to me that rebirth will also have to be understood as a psychological phenomenon. This is certainly not how it has been understood by Buddhist tradition. But to reinterpret a cosmological phenomenon as a psychological mechanism does seem to me to be consistent with the anti-speculative, almost medical attitude of the Buddha we meet in the Pāli discourses. It is not uncommon to see karma given a psychological reading along these lines, but the same treatment is rarely applied to rebirth, perhaps because it is harder to see how it might work. But if it is part of the Dharma’s task to explain how cultivation of the mind can bring about release from saṃsāra, then it seems to me that the components of cyclic existence will ultimately all have to be understood in psychological terms.

‘Psychological’ is a potentially misleading word here. The goal of modern empirical psychology is to explain human behavior, whereas Buddhist psychology is concerned with how latent mental processes construct our experience of the world and ourselves. In this respect Buddhist psychology has more in common with the Kantian project known as ‘transcendental psychology’ than with any branch of natural science. In making this comparison I mean to suggest that rebirth might be understood as a concept properly belonging to the domain of transcendental psychology, in much the same way Kant regards space and time. This is to understand rebirth as referring to an essential dynamic in the pre-conscious mental processes that produce our default, unenlightened experiences of self and world, and not some fact about the mechanics of the cosmos.

Of course the analogy will only go so far—for Kant the role performed by space and time is a kind of synthesis: they are means by which the mind brings unity to the raw data of experience. If rebirth is to be understood as a category of transcendental psychology then it will have to be one that moves in the opposite direction, pointing to a tendency of the mindstream towards disunity or fragmentation, with the wheel of dependent origination then providing a causal account of how this fragmentation is brought about.

The aim of this move is not to push rebirth over the line of scientific credibility, though if it had that effect it would not be unwelcome. What drives it is the requirement to make explicit how rebirth fits within the broader framework of the Dharma. Because rebirth clearly is essential to the Dharma—it is what gives saṃsāra its distinctive cyclical structure, it is a key link between the Buddhist moral project and the goal of awakening, and so on. A secular interpretation which discarded it would no longer be Buddhist, and (more importantly) it would deprive the Dharma of much of its resonance. But equally, to treat rebirth as something to be left in the too-difficult basket until you’re bought in enough to take it as part of the bundle is to ensure that resonance remains mysterious. What a psychological interpretation of rebirth promises is a means of clarifying exactly why rebirth must end with awakening, a clarity which will necessarily lead to a deeper grasp of what both concepts truly mean.

  1. “The Buddha’s teaching has many sides, and thus, from certain angles, it can be directly evaluated against our concern for our present well-being and happiness. Once we see that the practice of the teaching does indeed bring peace, joy, and inner security in this very life, this will inspire our trust and confidence in the Dhamma as a whole, including those aspects that lie beyond our present capacity for personal verification.” In the Buddha’s Words p85. [1] ↩︎

item.title: Two Flavours of Non-Duality
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description:
[A] dualism: a distinction drawn in terms that make essential relations between the distinguished items unintelligible.—Robert Brandom1

There is not the slightest difference between saṃsāra and nirvāṇa. There is not the slightest difference between nirvāṇa and saṃsāra.—Nāgārjuna2

Duality is an ambiguous word. Sometimes when we speak of duality we may just be indicating the presence of a difference. But at others we may intend something more specific and weighty, that distinctively metaphysical kind of difference more directly evoked by the term ‘dualism.’ These two senses of duality correspond to two ways of understanding subject-object duality and what it might mean to overcome it. The first sees it as a matter of overcoming the illusion of difference between subject and object. The second locates the illusion elsewhere, in whatever it is that makes a real difference between subject and object show up as a false dualism.
item.content:encoded:
[A] dualism: a distinction drawn in terms that make essential relations between the distinguished items unintelligible.—Robert Brandom1

There is not the slightest difference between saṃsāra and nirvāṇa. There is not the slightest difference between nirvāṇa and saṃsāra.—Nāgārjuna2

Duality is an ambiguous word. Sometimes when we speak of duality we may just be indicating the presence of a difference. But at others we may intend something more specific and weighty, that distinctively metaphysical kind of difference more directly evoked by the term ‘dualism.’ These two senses of duality correspond to two ways of understanding subject-object duality and what it might mean to overcome it. The first sees it as a matter of overcoming the illusion of difference between subject and object. The second locates the illusion elsewhere, in whatever it is that makes a real difference between subject and object show up as a false dualism.

The first wants to understand the true relationship between subject and object on the model of metaphysical identity. In the final analysis subject and object are unified in a single, ultimate substrate of reality, conceived of as undifferentiated in itself. According to this view subject-object duality manifests as a kind of cognitive veil, a conceptual net projected over the raw experience of reality, carving it up into all sorts of illusory differences, including the perceiving subject’s own difference from the reality it perceives. The experience of non-duality is imagined as a lifting or piercing of this veil, a cessation of conceptual activity in which the perceiving subject attains direct experience of undifferentiated reality, and consequently experiences itself as undifferentiated from it.

The second framework appeals to the notion of reification. According to this view subject and object are indeed different, in the sense that there is no single underlying substance or substrate in which they are ultimately unified. What is at issue in subject-object duality is rather a tendency we have to reify subject and object—to posit them as distinct substances—not just in our theories but also in our cognitive reflexes. The practical significance of this reification is that subject and object are treated as self-subsisting things which could in principle exist independently. But according to this picture the relation between them is in truth a relation of co-constitution, and in this respect they are logically inseparable. The corresponding experience of non-duality is one in which the reifying cognitive reflex is suspended, in which subject and object can show up in consciousness without automatically being imputed with the kind of intrinsic substantiality that makes thinkable their coming apart.

The first view is often associated with Advaita Vedanta, while the second is recognisably Buddhist. How non-duality is actually understood within these traditions is a complex issue, and is not something I want to get into in this post. The aim here is just to contrast two distinct models that hover in the background of discussions of non-duality—sometimes in a shadowy kind of way—without getting too bogged down in hermeneutics. I’ll refer to them simply as the identity model and the reification model.

The reification model is certainly harder to get a grip on than the identity model, but there are clear tensions between the two. Firstly, the reification model recognises the reality of difference, while the identity model posits that all differences are illusory from the perspective of ultimate reality. The reification model therefore avoids the trouble that the identity model is going to have explaining the difference between everyday truth and everyday falsehood, since if conceptual cognition always involves imposing a false difference then this implies that all cognition is in error. Secondly, the identity model doesn’t actually address the issue of reification—it merely displaces it by positing a single reified substance in which the difference of subject and object is annulled. If the reification of reality presents a problem in itself, over and above its tendency to harden differences into dualisms, the identity model will have to answer to it.

Given that something is indeed at stake in the difference between these two frameworks, how might we get a better grasp on the reification model and the corresponding experience of non-duality? I find it useful to think of this in terms of a theatre analogy. In naturalistic forms of theatre the universe depicted on stage is presented as a self-contained totality. The audience exist at an Archimedean point outside it, looking in on a world that cannot look back. This separation between the world of the stage and that of the audience is a kind of reification, not purely cognitive in this case but a product of social convention, helped along by lighting and stage design. This artifice conspires to construct the world of the stage as self-subsistent and separate, bracketing the true state of affairs: that audience and stage are constituted as such only in their relationship to one another.

But this situation can be disrupted, as when a character addresses the audience directly. By breaking the fourth wall the character in effect places a demand on the audience to recognise them as a social agent within their world, undermining its separation from the world of the stage. This moment contains a glimpse of what it means for a structure of reification to collapse, and so what this experience of non-duality might really be like. This analogy encourages us to imagine subject-object duality as something like a staging effect, one that occurs in the process of cognition itself, where the construction of the object of perception as a distinct self-subsisting unity is just the other face of the displacement of the perceiving subject to an observational viewpoint outside its sphere of involvement. The construction of the world as ‘objective’ (in the sense of being articulated into distinct self-subsisting objects) is at the same time an artificial suspension of the subject’s participation within it.

Still we might wonder, how exactly does this picture differ from the identity model? After all, breaking a fourth wall sounds remarkably similar to piercing a veil. But the important difference is that breaking a fourth wall is not about coming into some kind of ‘direct’ contact with the object, insofar as ‘direct’ means unmediated by concepts, as if the character were to dissolve into a pure flux of consciousness the moment they addressed you. The person on stage remains as they are, their attributes left in tact. What has been disrupted is the background of convention which positions them in their role.

In this experience of non-duality we should expect no change in the content of cognition, only a modification of its form. If we can think of this as a species of illusion, then it is not the kind in which the object appears other than it really is. It is instead the kind of illusion which, once shed, only reveals the object as it always had been. Not the lifting of the veil to reveal what is behind it, but the realisation that the veil was never there to begin with.

To put it like this is to risk recycling a platitude, but I hope these reflections can help put some meat on the bones of this old cliché. If subject-object duality is realised not by a conceptual veil that obscures the true reality, but through an artificial displacement of the subject to a position outside the world of the object, then the experience of non-duality need not involve some kind of unification with undifferentiated ultimate reality, or indeed anything so lofty. Instead it will consist in something more subtle: the loss of a certain observational distance, after which the subject will find themselves for the first time in the world they have always occupied, with all its familiar differentiations. Only this time it will be a world in which they are involved not accidentally but essentially, in virtue of their very constitution.

  1. A Spirit of Trust p11. [1] ↩︎

  2. Mūlamadhyamakakārikā Chapter 25, verse 19. [2] ↩︎
